profile.name: Zhiping Lan
profile.tagline: Senior Software Engineer | Tech Lead | Scalable Web Systems
profile.bio:
ExpediaGroup | GE HealthCare | EPAM Systems | Teleopti

A senior software engineer and tech lead with 10+ years of experience building scalable web applications and leading technical initiatives across travel, healthcare, retail, workforce management, and AI-related products.

Throughout my career, I've had the opportunity to work with leading organizations including ExpediaGroup, GE HealthCare, EPAM Systems, Teleopti, and FDT AI, where I've built and launched partner platforms, enterprise dashboards, medical-device asset systems, e-commerce experiences, scheduling products, and internal tooling that empowered multiple teams.

My expertise spans AWS Cloud, CI/CD pipelines, React and TypeScript ecosystems, frontend architecture, technical mentoring, and collaborative partnerships with product and design teams to deliver impactful solutions.

recentBlogs.title: AI-First UI Components
recentBlogs.summary: To be done: a post about AI-First UI components and how they can extend traditional interfaces.
recentBlogs.content:
Context

In the era of AI, everyone is talking about how to build an all-in-one chat web application like ChatGPT. However, that will be a huge breaking change for users and developers. Instead, I am more interested in how we can build AI-First UI components that can be easily integrated into existing applications, empowering traditional UI with AI capabilities without requiring a complete overhaul.

To Be Done

This article is still to be done. The final version will cover the AI-First UI component approach, implementation details, and practical integration notes.

recentBlogs.title: Building a Shared Charting Library Across Multiple Expedia Teams
recentBlogs.summary: How a reusable charting library reduced duplication, standardized data visualization, and improved delivery speed across internal teams.
recentBlogs.content:
Context

At Expedia Group, I built a custom charting library that was adopted across multiple teams. The original problem was repeated effort: teams were solving similar data visualization needs with inconsistent implementations, styling, and maintenance costs.

What I Want To Expand Later

The design constraints that pushed us toward a shared library
The API design decisions that helped adoption
How we balanced flexibility with consistency

Draft Story

The core goal was not just to build components, but to create a reliable internal platform for product teams shipping metrics-heavy experiences. By packaging shared charting patterns into a reusable library, we reduced duplication and made dashboards easier to build and maintain.

This project also created a common language for frontend teams. Instead of rebuilding chart behavior for each surface, teams could focus on business logic and product outcomes.

Placeholder For Results

I want to add more concrete adoption numbers, before-and-after implementation examples, and lessons learned from rollout.

recentBlogs.title: Shopping Website Progressive Filtering
recentBlogs.summary: A filtering design that supports both server-side rendering and client-side updates for product listing pages.
recentBlogs.content:
Context

At EPAM Systems, I worked on a shopping website experience where filtering needed to work well in both server-rendered and browser-rendered flows. The main challenge was keeping the page fast and shareable without sacrificing interactive behavior once JavaScript was available.

SSR Flow

On the server side, the page reads the selected filter state from the URL query parameter. That allows the product listing page to be built with the chosen filters already applied, so the initial response is usable immediately and can be indexed or shared as a normal URL.

The server then assembles the product listing items dynamically based on that filter state. This keeps the markup aligned with the selected filters instead of relying on the browser to fix the page after load.

Client-Side Flow

Once the browser takes over, JavaScript re-renders both the filter component and the product list component. When a user changes a filter, the interface updates the displayed items without requiring a full page reload.

This gives the experience a progressive enhancement model: the server delivers the first useful view, and the client adds smoother interactivity after hydration.

Why This Design Worked

The design kept SSR and client-side rendering in sync through the same filter state. That reduced edge cases, preserved a good initial load experience, and still let the user refine the listing fluidly in the browser.

It was also a practical architecture choice for commerce pages, where filter URLs need to be stable, shareable, and easy to rebuild from the incoming request.

recentBlogs.title: Shopping Website Image Loading Optimization
recentBlogs.summary: How I improved image loading with placeholders, multiple image sources, and a reusable img-src pattern.
recentBlogs.content:
Context

At EPAM Systems, I worked on a shopping website frontend and spent time improving how product imagery loaded across the site. The goal was to keep pages visually stable while still delivering the right image for the user's device and network conditions.

The Problem

Commerce pages depend heavily on images, but large assets can slow down rendering and make the interface feel unstable during load. We needed a pattern that could show something immediately, avoid layout shifts, and still pick the best available source for each viewport.

The Approach

I introduced a placeholder image strategy so the UI could reserve space and show a low-cost visual before the final asset was ready. That reduced the sense of jank while the page was loading.

For the actual asset delivery, I used a multi-source pattern driven by an img-src tag so the page could describe more than one candidate image. The browser could then choose the most appropriate source while the frontend kept a consistent markup contract.

What This Solved

This approach improved perceived performance, reduced visual instability, and made image behavior more predictable across the product experience. It also created a cleaner implementation model for other frontend surfaces that needed responsive image handling.

Why It Matters

This work was a good example of balancing polish and performance. In e-commerce, image delivery is part of the user experience, not just an asset-loading detail. Small improvements here made the site feel faster and more intentional.

pastProjects.title: Burberry E-commerce Experience
pastProjects.summary: At EPAM Systems, I contributed to Burberry's e-commerce platform through frontend engineering, performance improvements, rendering optimization, and internal knowledge sharing.
pastProjects.content:
Overview

At EPAM Systems, I contributed to Burberry's e-commerce web application, a customer-facing platform that supported the brand's online retail experience. My work focused on frontend engineering and performance improvement, with an emphasis on building a fast, polished, and reliable experience for users.

Frontend and Performance Work

I worked on frontend improvements that helped enhance the overall responsiveness and efficiency of the application. This included optimizing how frontend assets were delivered, improving rendering performance, and supporting a more scalable server-side setup for the web application.

I also contributed to media delivery improvements by applying responsive image techniques, helping ensure that users received appropriately sized assets for different devices and viewing conditions. These efforts supported better loading behavior and a smoother browsing experience.

Rendering and User Experience

Part of my work involved improving server-side rendering to support both user experience and search visibility. This helped strengthen the initial page experience for content-heavy commerce pages and contributed to better overall frontend performance.

Team Contribution

Beyond product delivery, I contributed to team development through onboarding support, knowledge sharing, and technical interviews. I also delivered internal JavaScript Fundamentals lessons, helping colleagues strengthen core language knowledge and frontend development practices.

Why This Experience Matters

This experience was meaningful because it combined hands-on product work with broader engineering impact. It was not only about delivering features, but also about improving performance, maintainability, and team capability. It reflects the kind of work I enjoy most: building user-focused web experiences while contributing to a stronger engineering culture.

Highlights

Contributed to frontend improvements for a global e-commerce experience
Supported performance optimization across rendering and asset delivery
Applied responsive image techniques to improve media loading efficiency
Helped strengthen engineering practices through onboarding, teaching, and interviews

pastProjects.title: Expedia PartnerCentral
pastProjects.summary: Senior Software Engineer at ExpediaGroup, leading frontend initiatives on Expedia PartnerCentral — a property management platform for global hotel partners — with a focus on dashboards, data visualization, and platform-wide tooling.
pastProjects.content:
PartnerCentral

As a Senior Software Engineer at ExpediaGroup (April 2021–present), I reported to the Director of Engineering and worked closely with PMs and UX designers to shape product direction and system architecture. I acted as technical lead across multiple initiatives, mentored junior developers, organized coding training sessions, and participated in hiring processes. I also facilitated scrum rituals including sprint planning, backlog grooming, and retrospectives.

Key achievements include:

Custom charting library — Built a charting library adopted across multiple teams, standardizing data visualization practices and eliminating duplication.
Dynamic metrics dashboard — Delivered a metrics dashboard for Expedia PartnerCentral that improved visibility into key product performance indicators for internal stakeholders.
UI toolkit integration — Led the integration of a new UI toolkit into legacy systems via a reusable NPM package adopted department-wide.

Tech Stack: TypeScript, React, Node.js, ECharts, Webpack, GraphQL, NPM, AWS Lambda, API Gateway, ECS, GitHub Actions, Jenkins, Spinnaker

pastProjects.summary: Built an Asset Performance Management system for medical devices in GE HealthCare's healthcare business unit.
pastProjects.content: This project combined enterprise engineering discipline with product reliability requirements in a healthcare environment. It also reflected my broader technical leadership through peer mentoring, interviewing, onboarding support, and steady delivery.

pastProjects.title: Teleopti Workforce Scheduling Tools
pastProjects.summary: Built enterprise scheduling tools at Teleopti, including seat planning and automated shift scheduling systems.
pastProjects.content: This work included AngularJS- and KnockoutJS-based product development, enterprise workflow design, product demos, and close collaboration with an international team using XP practices like pair programming.
